data.title: Freeport-McMoRan Q3 Earnings: The Market Is Not Impressed
data.description:
Earlier this week, Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (NYSE:FCX) reported its Q3 2024 results and the market did not seem impressed. The stock is actually down by 1.3% over the past 5 trading days and is up nearly 13% on a year-to-date basis at a time when the S&P 500 delivered returns of almost twice that rate.

Although the market did not react to the earnings release, there were some noteworthy developments over the past 3-month period.

Earnings improved by 16% year-on-year on the back of higher copper's average realized price as copper production fell. Thus, FCX's stock remains driven by changes in copper price and operational developments within the company are just a nuisance at this point.

All As Expected

For the most part, the earnings call involved topics that FCX investors are well-aware of. From the ongoing expansion in Indonesia and the expected strong demand for copper due to electrification to the need to improve efficiencies in the U.S. to tackle the impact of lower grades and lastly growth opportunities from the company's leach initiatives.

All these topics are important for long-term investors, but nothing materially has changed during the quarter. For example, incremental copper production for the company's highly innovative leaching process increased by nearly 70% during the quarter.

At Freeport, we benefit from a large reserve and resource position with near term, medium term and longer term embedded growth options. The high potential innovative leach technologies are delivering results and we've several ongoing initiatives to add scale and low cost copper volumes in our Americas business. In the first nine months of 2024, the incremental copper production from our leach initiative was nearly 70% higher than the comparable period last year, and we have additional projects underway to build scale and improve on our US cost position.

Source: FCX Q3 2024 Earnings Transcript

An impressive achievement to tackle the problem of low-grade ores, but that was already expected, and thus the copper sales outlook did not change.

Expected sale volumes of gold and molybdenum also remained unchanged during the quarter, as the price of the precious metal has skyrocketed in recent months. These two segments, however, remain a small fraction of FCX sales.

Management has also announced a slight improvement in cash costs from the July estimate, which brings the FY 2024 outlook roughly in-line with the one provided at the beginning of the year.

We also have updated our estimates for our unit net cash cost. We expect our average for 2024 to approximate $1.58 per pound. That is below our July estimate of $1.63 per pound and similar to our guidance at the start of the year of $1.60 per pound.

Source: FCX Q3 2024 Earnings Transcript

Overall, the quarterly earnings release did not offer any material changes to FCX's near-term outlook, which now puts the emphasis back on copper prices.

All Eyes On Copper Prices

The long-term thesis for copper prices is indeed attractive. Demand for copper is expected to increase on the back of electrification trends and the need for more investments within electrical grids around the globe.

The problem for FCX shareholders, however, is that owning the stock is associated with many other operational and financial risks that could easily distort future returns, even if eventually copper prices skyrocket.

FCX management continues to expect a tightly balanced market in the near term and deficit conditions beyond that, which creates an appealing narrative around copper mining stocks. The near-term risks, however, are significant, and they highlight the need for almost perfect market timing when trying to take advantage of long-term trends through mining stocks.

Copper prices are very sensitive to changes in expectations about the global economy, and this, in my view, makes FCX uninvestable at the moment. The risks for the global economy continue to pile-up, and forecasting future actions by central banks around the globe is a futile exercise.

(...) where prices traded, they largely followed macro sentiment, which weighed global economic data and economic pressures in China against actions by the Fed and other central banks to cut interest rates and the potential for large economic stimulus in China.

Source: FCX Q3 2024 Earnings Transcript

The yield curve continues to steepen which historically is a strong signal of a potential recession ahead and the Conference Board Leading Economic Index (LEI) is once again signalling woes for the U.S. economy ahead.

The unprecedented stimulus measures in China are also not a very encouraging signal for the health of the world's second-largest economy.

The copper to gold ratio is also near all-time lows, which a clear indication of the current risks for the global economy.

The reason why the copper to gold ratio is so important is because higher copper prices are associated with optimistic expectations about the economy, while higher gold prices are a strong signal of elevated risks for the global monetary system.

That is why, historically, we have seen a strong relationship between the copper to gold ratio and the 10-year bond yields, which fall during periods of time when economic stimulus is needed.

This relationship, however, has been broken as yields skyrocketed in recent year and are now at their highest levels since 2007.

In a nutshell, copper and gold prices are signalling more troubles ahead for the economy as long-term rates continue to increase after years of record-low levels.

All that creates significant short to medium term risks for the economy and even though the long-term thesis for copper prices might seem attractive, the downside risk is still too high to ignore.

Conclusion

The market did not seem impressed with Freeport-McMoRan's third quarter results for FY 2024. In spite of some positive developments during the quarter, the outlook remains unchanged. Progress is made in certain areas, such as Indonesia's expansion and costs, but for the time being investors remain laser focused on movements in copper prices. In that regard, risks still outweigh potential benefits.

data.title: Apollo Investor Day's Mysteries
data.description:
Apollo Global (NYSE:APO) held its Investor Day on October 1. The presentation was more than four hours long and included 200+ slides. Instead of reviewing it, I will limit myself to only several points that seemed intriguing to me.

Before we proceed, I want to remind you that there are two ways to invest in Apollo - either through its common stock APO or through its mandatory convertible preferred stock (NYSE:APO.PR.A).

The latter pays $3.375 per annum dividends and will be converted into APO on 7/31/2026. As long as APO trades above $98.97, the conversion ratio will be 0.5052 of APO shares per one APO.PR.A. Since the market is pretty certain about the "as long as" condition, the preferred stock trades slightly above 0.5052*APO (~ $72.75 at the time of writing) due to its downside protection in the unlikely case of APO's deep drop and to compensate for its higher dividend.

Newcomers can review my multiple posts on Apollo for the company's description, which I will skip here.

The Market Responds To Investor Day

Below is APO's chart from SA:

My modest contribution to the slide above is two arrows: the stock's drop on Aug 1 - the day of Apollo's Q2 earnings release, and the stock's jump on Oct 1 - the Investor Day.

On August 2, I published "Apollo's stock punished for a less than stellar quarter - nothing to worry about". The title describes what happened, though the market's reaction was out of proportion. The stock started climbing back until it jumped on Investor Day and has further advanced since, trading at an all-time high.

And here comes my question: what impressed investors so much on Investor Day?

In my August 2nd publication, I estimated Apollo's intrinsic value at $90-123. Granted, I strive to be conservative with my estimates. Still, the quick APO's ascend to $145, i.e. significantly above my fair value range, surprised me more than the drop after the Q2 release because I did not discover anything unexpected in the presentation except for some secondary details.

Investors were more likely to respond to the strategic plan of CEO Marc Rowan's presentation than to anything else. So, we will focus on it, and below is the central slide of his presentation.

The targets on the right side of the slide seemed very expected to me and in my posts, I often shared these expectations, emphasizing the most important figure of ~20% FRE growth. This number is the most influential on the stock price and that is why it is at the very top. Other numbers, besides SRE growth, serve only to provide for FRE growth. (By the way, SRE growth of 10% seems rather modest to me in the probable case of rating agencies further upgrading Athene.)

Back to the question I posed earlier. In my opinion (and I am not confident here), there are two reasons for the stock's jump - a microreason and a macro reason.

The microreason is presented on the least important slide of Mr. Rowan's presentation:

The slide explains relatively modest Q2 numbers caused by hedging of floating rate securities. In 2023, they overperformed, but are likely to underperform once interest rates start going down. Once Mr. Rowan attributed hedging costs to 2023 instead of Q2 2024 when they were incurred, Q2 numbers started looking good.

All this number-shuffling seems rather meaningless to me, especially since this issue was discussed in detail on Athene's Q2 earnings call! (Athene's earnings calls are separate from Apollo's and present a lot of valuable info.) Perhaps, the market is not that efficient after all...

The macro reason is revealed below:

The slide illustrates the total addressable market for opportunities in front of Apollo and its peers. Nobody can be certain about the precision of the numbers but their enormity impresses nonetheless. Even though I was familiar with all these opportunities, until Investor Day, I had never seen them succinctly packed on one slide. Perhaps, it helped the investing crowd to recognize what might lie ahead. Big alt managers are likely to grab an out-of-proportion fraction of these immense opportunities, as has already happened:

Private investments and the index

A part of Mr. Rowan's presentation was devoted to the comparison of public and private equity investments. He was rather critical of public markets. Read this, for example:

10 stocks are 39% of the S&P, 4 stocks have determined 100% of their returns for the last few years. And I jokingly say sometimes, we levered the entire retirement of America to Nvidia's performance. It just doesn't seem smart. We're going to fix this and we are in the process of fixing it...

The 8,000 public companies that used to form diversified portfolios are now 4,000 public companies. Fewer than 100 companies go public every year, more than 100 companies go private every year. We take for granted in the technology ecosystem that companies like OpenAI or Spotify can stay private a very long time and raise equity.

His ideas are clear and widely recognized: several big companies dominate the market, the market cannot deliver anything but beta, mutual funds underperform, and private investments will deliver alpha. Fair enough.

But how is it compatible with the following slide from his presentation?

Suddenly, we realize that despite denouncing the search for alpha in public equities, Mr. Rowan acknowledges that public Apollo has delivered this alpha and is hopeful that this will remain the case. He did not state the latter directly, but I think clearly implied it. Otherwise, his investment thesis for Apollo does not make much sense.

Bizarre responses by the market to Apollo's messages that we discussed in the previous section confirm that public markets, despite AI and everything else, are far from perfectly efficient. Perhaps, one can still invest at appropriate moments into the public operators of private investments (i.e. Apollo and other alt managers) and outperform the index? The simple fact that during two months from Aug 1 to Oct 1, APO jumped almost 50% without any significant news, confirms that public appraisal of alt managers is hardly efficient.

Conclusion

There was one other slightly mysterious moment in Mr. Rowan's presentation. It is well known that KKR (KKR) and Brookfield (BN) (BAM) cloned Apollo's insurance strategy. Mr. Rowan never mentioned them, but still shared his opinion with us. First, he mentioned that one of Apollo's competitors did everything well and then, speaking about Athene's competitive advantages, stated the following:

But suffice it to say, if you do not have ratings and you do not have service and you do not have this, what you're doing is you're buying secondary blocks of business with degraded surrender charges and degraded protection, and you are paying more for them than originating new business. Not a great way to run...

If you don't have these things, and most people who are entering the business and most traditional companies don't have any of these things, what do you do? Well, what you do is you take assets and liabilities to Cayman. You put up $0.50 on capital. You avoid a bunch of rules. And that's what we're watching. Any time someone tells you they're taking their business to Cayman, read that is short lived and lacking in any of these things.

Whom do you think he had in mind when dropping these words? I think it is rather obvious - all you need is to generalize Cayman, which, of course, is not a geographical name here, into other offshore locations such as Bermuda, for example...

After the jump, Apollo stock has become less attractive and is outside my fair value range. Shortly, it will deliver Q3 results, but they may not be stellar either due to the same reason - floating fixed income hedging. At least, it was directly mentioned on the last Athene earnings call. In Q4, the company is expected to resume back to normal, which means quick growth.

I remain optimistic about Apollo in the long term but will change my rating to "Hold" due to quick appreciation.

data.title: Turn Around Tuesday Comes Late
data.description:
Overview

It is ironic that a few months ago, many wanted to sell the dollar because the Republican president and vice president candidates said they wanted a weaker dollar. With the election drawing near and the race very tight, there has been a surge in the betting markets of a Trump-Vance victory, and this has corresponded with the dollar's dramatic rise. US rates held on the lion's share of their gains despite the sharpest loss in the S&P 500 since early September to cap the three-day slide. Still, the greenback is consolidating today, and seeing some of its recent gains pared against all the G10 currencies. And all but a few Asia Pacific currencies and the Russian ruble are firmer, too.

Corrective forces are also evident in global stock and bond markets. Asia Pacific equities were mostly lower, following the losses in the US yesterday. But helped by stronger earnings reports (see Tesla (TSLA), for example) US S&P 500 and Nasdaq futures are 0.4%-0.7% higher and Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is also snapping a three-day drop. Bonds are being bought too. Japan and Australian 10-year benchmark yields slipped almost two basis points, while European yields are off 3-4 bps. The UK Gilts are an exception. There, the yield is up nearly three basis points. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is off more than five basis points to 4.19%, leaving it still 11 bps higher on the week. Gold appeared to post a key reversal yesterday by setting a record high and then closing below the previous day's low. However, there has been no follow-through selling, and gold has recovered from $2715.55 at yesterday's close to approach $2740 today. December WTI is at an eight-day high, pushing above $72.20.

Asia Pacific

Since the low set on September 30 near JPY141.65, the US dollar has surged more than 8% against the yen, which, if sustained, would be the largest monthly gain since August 1995. From early June through early July, the greenback rallied about 4.8% and Japanese officials climbed the intervention ladder to large scale, initially unconfirmed intervention. We argue that what has made BOJ intervention appear effective is that officials sold dollars in a falling US rate environment. This was true in intervention in September 2022 and earlier this year. A consensus appears to have been formed that no matter the US election results, a larger budget deficit will result and higher US inflation. The US 10-year yield has risen by nearly 65 bps since mid-September to almost 4.25%. Although, we argue that due to its prior views and positioning, the market exaggerated the significance of the stronger-than-expected September employment data. Even if we are right that the October report will be more consistent with the underlying trend of a gradual slowing in job growth, the proximity of the US election and related concerns may prevent a pullback in US yields. Nevertheless, with the weaker yen no longer supporting Japanese equities, verbal intervention should be expected. Meanwhile, neither the Japanese nor Australian markets typically react much to the preliminary October PMI, and today is true to form. While Japan's manufacturing PMI remained below the 50 boom/bust level for the fourth consecutive month, the composite has been above 50 consistently this year, but for June. In October, it fell to 49.4 from 52.0. In its new economic projections, the IMF shaved its GDP forecast for Japan to 0.3% from 0.4%. In contrast, Australia's composite PMI remained below 50 for the second consecutive month (49.8 from 49.6). Despite the weakness of the yen, there is practically no chance of a Bank of Japan rate hike next week. The Reserve Bank of Australia meets on November 5, and it is unlikely to be swayed by today's PMI.

The dollar has surged by about 3% against the yen this week. It has nearly met the (61.8%) retracement of the intervention/US rate-inspired decline from the multiyear-high in early July (~JPY162) to the low in mid-September (briefly below JPY140). Speculators in the futures market swung from a net short position of 185k contracts in early July to net long 66k contracts in late September. They have been liquidating over the past few weeks, and price action is both a cause and effect of trend followers and momentum traders bailing out. There has been hardly a peep from Japanese officials about the price action. Both Finance Minister Kato and BOJ Governor Ueda had opportunities in the past 24 hours to comment on the yen but refrained. The strong three-day rally has stalled, and the dollar is pulling back from almost JPY153.20, yesterday's high, and has slipped below JPY152 in Europe. Nearby support may be in the JPY151.25-40 area. That shelf the Australian dollar was forging around $0.6655? It was taken out yesterday. The Aussie was sold to almost $0.6620 and settled below the 200-day moving average for the first time since mid-August. It is better bid today and has recovered to $0.6660 before pausing. There is a large option at $0.6605 (~A$1.55 bln) that expires today. The relentless rise of the dollar and yen slump is softening the Chinese yuan, even if there is greater foreign demand for Chinese shares. The dollar approached CNH7.1470 yesterday. As the dollar pulled back against the yen today, it has also pulled back against the yuan and approached CNH7.1165. The PBOC fix has been creeping up. It was set at CNY7.1286 today (CNY7.1245 yesterday).

Europe

The eurozone and UK's preliminary October PMI were uninspiring. The eurozone composite ticked up to 49.7 from 49.6. It was the second consecutive month below 50. So far, the year's peak was in May (52.2). Last October it was at 46.5. Germany's flash PMI was firmer and the composite rose to 48.4 from 47.5. The French results were softer, and the composite PMI fell to 47.3 from 48.6. The swaps market has about a 40% chance that the ECB cuts rates by a half-point at the December meeting, which, practically unchanged on the day, is slightly lower than at the end of last week. It still seems exaggerated. That said, another negative print on CPI (due next week) could change the assessment. The swaps market has slightly less than 125 bps of cuts through H1 25. In comparison, the Fed funds futures is discounting about 108 bps from the Federal Reserve. The swaps market has 112 bps of easing by the Bank of England over that period. The UK's preliminary October preliminary composite PMI slipped to 51.7 from 52.6 in September. It has not been below 50 since October 2023 when it was 48.7.

The euro took another leg lower yesterday and frayed the uptrend line connecting the April and June lows but managed to settle slightly above it (~$1.0765). It recovered to nearly $1.0810 in the European morning, which seemed to have exhausted the buying and the intraday momentum indicators warn of the risk of a pullback, with initial support in the $1.0770-80 area. There are options for 1.35 bln euros that expire today at $1.0755. Sterling frayed its trendline connecting the April and August lows found near $1.2910 yesterday, which met the (50%) retracement of this year's gains around $1.2930. Yesterday's low held today, and sterling recovered to $1.2975. Its intraday momentum indicators are more constructive than the euro, and the session high may not be in place. It may draw closer to $1.30.

America

US rates and the dollar are unlikely moved by today's data. It consists of the preliminary October PMI, where manufacturing has been below 50 for the past three months, weekly jobless claims (overshadowed by next week's national employment report), and September new home sales (existing home sales unexpectedly fell for the second consecutive month). Instead, the market is focused on the dramatic rise in US rates on ideas regardless of the election outcome, supply of Treasuries will increase, and inflation will rise. The measure of bond market volatility (MOVE) is at new highs for the year. The Bank of Canada delivered the 50 bp cut that was largely discounted, though it did not prevent the Canadian dollar from extending its losses. Its economic forecasts were unchanged from July. It expected the economy to grow by 2.1% in 2025 with 2.2% CPI. Governor Macklem characterized the economy as "excess supply," with "soft" labor market. Mexico reports inflation for the first half of October. The weaker peso may have lifted the headline inflation by the most in three months (~0.4%), The core rate has typically been firmer in the first half of a month than the second half. This pattern has held in all but three months since the start of last year. The swaps market has about a 1-in-3 chance of a cut discounted over the next month. The meeting is November 14.

The combination of a 50 bp Bank of Canada rate cut and the broader risk-off environment weighed on the Canadian dollar yesterday, but it slipped around 0.15%, making it the strongest of the G10 currencies. Still, the greenback reached new two-and-half-month highs near CAD1.3865. It is consolidating today and is likely to find support ahead of CAD1.3775. As the Loonie was the strongest in the G10, the Mexican peso was the strongest among the emerging market currencies yesterday, rising 0.5%. The greenback initially reached almost MXN20.10 before reversing lower and falling through MXN19.86. It is the third time in the last five sessions that the dollar traded above MXN20.00 on an intraday basis, and has been unable to close above it once. The greenback held above Tuesday's low (~MXN19.77) yesterday but has taken it out today. It fell to almost MXN19.7550 before finding bids in the European morning. Nearby resistance may be near MXN19.85.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Only Second Bank Failure This Year
data.description:
There has been a second bank failure in 2024.

Yes, so far...that's all.

And, this bank failure looks like there was something dishonest going on.

The failure was First National Bank of Lindsay, of Lindsay, Oklahoma. On June 30, 2024, it had $108 million of assets.

This was not the usual bank failure.

This was an unexpected and sudden closure.

The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency said it closed First National after finding “false and deceptive bank records and other information suggesting fraud.” The OCC added that the bank “was in an unsafe or unsound condition to transact business” and that its assets were less than its obligations.

A lot of uninsured depositors stand to lose a lot of money.

The OCC is bringing in the Justice Department to investigate the situation.

The interesting thing to me is that this is only the second bank closing this year.

The other failure was in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

Republic First Bank was quite a bit bigger than the First National Bank of Lindsay; it had $6 billion in assets.

Also, the uninsured depositors of Republic First Bank did not face the same problem that faced the uninsured depositors of First National Bank of Lindsay.

The bank that acquired Republic First Bank assumed all of the deposits of the bank...including the uninsured ones. One could say that it was a pretty clean takeover and that although the bank failed, the primary reason for the failure was the changes that had taken place in interest rates.

The major point that I want to make, however, is not the bank failure itself, but the fact that through roughly ten months in 2024, there have only been two bank failures so far in the calendar year!

This, to me, says a lot about what the Federal Reserve is doing and what it has been doing over the past 2 1/2 years or so.

Over the past 2 1/2 years, the Federal Reserve has been conducting a policy of monetary tightening. The specific policy approach it has been following it titled "quantitative tightening."

For the past 2 1/2 years or so, the Federal Reserve has overseen a reduction in its securities portfolio.

The reduction has been steady and expected.

The banking system has been prepared for the Fed's actions, announced in advance of execution.

The Fed has reduced its securities portfolio by more than $1.0 trillion.

The Federal Reserve had purchased a lot more securities than just $1.0 trillion as it responded to the impacts of the Covid-19 pandemic and subsequent recession.

And, current plans are for the Fed to continue reducing the size of the securities portfolio into the near future.

But, the point is that the Fed has been reducing the size of its securities portfolio for 2 1/2 years and has announced that it will continue to program further into the future.

So, in the almost 10 months of this year, and after almost thirty months preceding this year's 10 months, only two banks have failed.

I am calling attention to this outcome because it is a result of the monetary policy strategy devised by former Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke and speaks to some of the strengths of this particular policy strategy.

In the past, the Federal Reserve has attempted to reverse periods in which excessive amounts of liquidity have been provided to the banking system, the central bank has moved rapidly and adjusted aggressively to get the banking system back into a "more normal" situation.

The efforts to move quickly and reverse the monetary injection generally caused a major disruption of the banking system along with many bank failures.

The "new" Bernanke program has apparently avoided all of this kind of disruption.

Mr. Bernanke made sure that bank management's knew what the Fed was trying to do, knew that the adjustments would occur over an extended period so that the banks would have time to adjust more smoothly, and knew that they could trust the Fed in sticking to the program.

As a result of this approach the banking system appears to be adjusting to the Fed's efforts, the financial system seems to have accepted what the Federal Reserve is doing, and the economy seems to be rolling along at a pretty acceptable pace.

In other words, there have been very few bank failures.

The stock market has hit a very large number of new historic highs this year.

And, the economy looks as if it will grow by at least 2 1/2 percent in the coming year.

This is a fantastic record. And, the hope is that the Federal Reserve will be able to continue what it is doing into the near future with the same success.

Perhaps the most important lesson from this "new" monetary policy, a policy that focuses upon steadily moving quantities, is that monetary policy needs to be constructed in a way that avoids many, radical shifts over relatively short periods of time.

Focusing primarily on interest rate targets in the past resulted in financial markets and institutions that were volatile and that accepted many, many bank failures.

What the Federal Reserve has done with this "new" policy is to take a lot of the uncertainty out of what it will be doing for an extended period of time.

And, if the Federal Reserve can earn the trust of the banks, then banking adjustments will be smoother because the Federal Reserve policy will be known and will be lived up to.

Again, it is truly remarkable to me that the Federal Reserve has been conducting a policy of quantitative tightening for more than 30 months.

It is truly remarkable to me that the economy has been growing steadily during this time of quantitative tightening and is expected to continue to grow through this next year.

It is truly remarkable to me that inflation has dropped to a level that is approaching the Fed's target rate of inflation of 2.0 percent.

And, it is truly remarkable that the Fed was able to operate for a decade...the 2010s...using several periods of "quantitative easing" where the real economy grew at a compound rate of about 2.3 percent, and where inflation remained down at a compound rate of about 2.3 percent.

Furthermore, the banking system seems to be adjusting to this "new" approach to monetary policy and seems to be operating with fewer and fewer failures.

Not a bad outcome, Mr. Bernanke.

data.title: BHP Group: Dividend Play With Long-Term Upside
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome OBA Advisors as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

For investors who look for a long-term play that offers not only attractive dividend yields but also upside potential in capital gains, BHP Group Limited (NYSE:BHP) offers an opportunity that suits such an investment objective. The stock has an attractive yield of about 5% per annum as it stands. Meanwhile, the company is positioned in copper and iron ore productions globally, while continuing to expand its footprints such as potash mining in Canada. In our opinion, BHP is arguably an undervalued green energy play long term, owing to its copper portfolio, and a tactical play short term relating to China’s recent announcement of a massive economic stimulus initiative.

Overview

BHP Group is a global mining company headquartered in Melbourne, Australia. It is the world's largest mining company, by market capitalization. The highlights of its financial performance in FY2024 and outlook for FY2025 (by commodity) can be summarized (by mining segment) as follows -

Copper production reached the highest level in over 15 years, with an increase of 8.6% from previous year to a record level of 1.86 Mt (million tons Total copper revenue increased by US$2.5 billion to US$18.6 billion, with underlying EBITDA increased by US$1.9 billion to US$8.6 billion, driven by favorable price and foreign exchange impacts, as well as higher sales volume. The company also offers an outlook for total copper production in FY2025, estimating the figure will be somewhere between 1.85Mt and 2.05 Mt, representing a skewed upside potential in the coming year. As pointed out below, we believe that the demand for copper will remain strong in the long run, and accordingly, the above estimates ranging from flat to +10% appear to be well based.

Iron ore production also increased marginally, but nevertheless to a record level, at 260 Mt for the year. Total iron ore revenue reached US$28.0 billion, with an increase of US$3.1 billion from the previous year. The underlying EBITDA reached US$18.9 billion, with an increase of US$2.2 billion from the previous year, pursuant to higher average realized prices and higher sales volumes. The company, however, is estimating that the outlook for total iron ore production level in FY2025 is expected to remain range-bounded, between 250 Mt and 260 Mt. In the above connection, we should pay attention that BHP's marginal growth in the iron ore segment was achieved while China suffered economic downturn in its property sectors. We expect that BHP will continue to execute efficiently in regard to its iron ore strategy in the coming year.

Steelmaking coal production decreased whereas energy coal increased, with total coal revenue decreasing by US$3.3 billion to US$7.7 billion. The underlying EBITDA lowered by over 50% to US$2.3 billion from the preceding year, caused primarily by lower realized prices and lower sales volumes. Outlook for steelmaking coal and thermal coal productions in FY2025 is expected to be between 16.5 Mt and 19.0 Mt, and between 13 Mt and 15 Mt respectively. On a relative basis, the coal segment's contribution (vis-à-vis copper and iron ore segments) has dropped. Our view is that these estimates will not affect the bottom line materially in the foreseeable future.

Other Assets: Nickel revenue decreased by US$0.5 billion to US$1.5 billion in FY2024, reflecting lower average realized prices, with underlying EBITDA decreased by US$0.5 billion to a loss of US$0.3 billion. Furthermore, on 11 July 2024, the company decided to suspend operations temporarily till February 2027 at Western Australia Nickel. Potash development (known as the Jansen project) continued to track to plan, though the development cost has caused an underlying EBITDA loss of US$255 million in FY2024. The company expects to first production in FY2029.

On a consolidated basis, the company’s revenue increased 3.4% to US$55.7 billion in FY2024, with underlying EBITDA increased 3.8% to US$29.0 billion, with copper and iron ore mining each contributing 30% and 65% respectively in FY2024. Total dividend per share for the year amounted to US$2.92, representing a yield of approximately 5.0% (based on the closing price on Oct 23, 2024).

Market Dynamics

Key commodities produced by BHP include: copper, for electrification, and iron ore, for steel production. These two commodities together accounted for over 95% of the company’s revenues of FY2024. Hence, in our opinion, we need to pay special focus on these 2 key metals in order to analyze and understand the company's performance drivers and how these drivers can materially affect the share price of the company going forward.

Long-Term Trend – Copper Demand

BHP demonstrated its ability to capitalize on the growing demand for copper, basing on the reported increase in its copper production and contribution in FY2024. With this as a backdrop, going into FY2025 and beyond, it is important for investors to pay special attention to 2 macro trends – namely (i) clean energy expansion and (ii) artificial intelligence adoption, and ascertain whether BHP's copper segment can continue to be its growth driver for the years ahead.

Clean Energy Expansion: Back in April 2021, a GS research report, titled “Copper is the New Oil”, labelled copper as the “green” metal for transition to decarbonization. Copper price reacted positively in the following few weeks, rising from US$4.25 per lb from mid-April 2021 to US$4.77 by early May 2021, and after a volatile period of trading, reached an all-time high at US$5.08 per lb in May 2024. The metal is now trading at about US$4.42 per lb. The report continued to suggest that copper would be used to power the next generation of clean tech, and that copper’s demand from wind turbines, electric vehicles, solar panels, and energy storage would grow by 5.4 Mt in the base case or 8.7 Mt in the best case. More importantly, the report argued that there would be a supply and demand imbalance of 8.2 Mt by 2030, which would necessarily drive copper price to US$15,000 per ton (equivalent to about US$6.70 per lb), representing approximately 50% upside from the current level. BHP reported its average realized price for copper as US$3.98 per lb in FY2024, suggesting that there is a lot of upside potential to its stock side as the mining giant rides the wave of clean energy expansion. Not surprisingly, in early 2024, China, as the second-largest economy in the world, also announced that it would consider (and adopt) its energy industries, including manufacturing industries of solar panels, electric vehicles, and batteries, as strategic drivers for its economy. Incidentally, this announcement was made at a time when China’s economy was facing severe headwinds, and still struggling to re-ignite growth, and therefore, in our opinion, the announced strategy should not be interpreted as a stimulus measure, but rather was meant to be a long-term mandate for the country, reinforcing the “copper is the new oil” thesis.

Artificial Intelligence: Not only can electric vehicles (EVs), renewable energy systems and advanced technologies drive high demand on copper, but the recent race of AI adoptions in general and data centers in particular also lead the surge in copper demand, as copper serves an important role in getting electricity to these technologies. In our opinion, as AI adoption accelerates in the foreseeable future, the looming shortage of copper supply will be unavoidable. According to a forecast by BHP itself, data centers’ copper demand is expected to increase from less than 1% currently to around 6% to 7% by 2050. In our view, copper as a commodity will be increasingly important as AI technology progresses in the coming decades. From an investor's point of view, the key question is whether BHP will continue to be able to strengthen its position in the copper segment of the commodity market, notwithstanding that BHP owns one of the world’s largest copper mines in the Atacama Desert, Chile. In this connection, our opinion is that, while BHP has suffered temporary setbacks relating to its attempted acquisition of Anglo American (OTCQX:AAUKF), it will continue to benefit from the supply and demand imbalance of the “green” metal and, as the demand for copper continues to rise over the long run, its existing copper portfolio will continue to make meaningful contribution to the bottom line.

Short-Term Factor – Iron Ore Demand

For FY2024, regarding its iron ore segment, BHP achieved a marginal rise in production, and combined with increased revenues, suggested a stable demand, especially from steel-producing regions. Going forward into 2025, our view is that one new economic factor, namely the economic stimulus measures announced recently by China’s central bank (PBOC), may affect its stock price positively in the short term.

Economic Stimulus Measures: What exactly are the economic stimulus measures? Let's do a quick summary. Firstly, China’s central bank will cut the amount of cash that banks have to hold in reserve (known as reserve requirement ratio or RRR) by 50 basis points (half a percentage point) initially, with another cut possibly later this year. Secondly, lending banks will lower mortgage-debt servicing rate for existing mortgages and cut the minimum downpayment requirement to 15% on all types of homes. Thirdly, special “facilities” comprising a swap program initially sized at 500 billion yuan will be introduced to allow funds, insurers and brokers to access funds for buying publicly listed stocks. Fourthly, central bank loans will be provided to commercial banks to fund other customer entities’ share purchases and buybacks. Lastly, central banks will issue sovereign bonds to fund consumers and local government spending, and state-owned banks. The financial market has viewed the measures positively, though it is not so clear and less agreeable as to what extent the central bank’s measures will affect the Chinese economy. While the details of the measures are yet to be confirmed, one market view considered that the stimulus package was estimated to worth about 7.5 trillion yuan (US$1.07 trillion), which is equivalent to 6% of China’s GDP in 2024.

Iron Ore Demand Revival: Iron ore price surged from about US$80 per ton in Jan 2020 to an all-time high at US$220 in July 2021, before the metal returned to a trading range between US$80 and US$160. Currently, iron core is trading at about US$106. In our opinion, as and when China rolls out the full spectrum of the stimulus measures over the course of the coming months, they will likely propel iron ore prices to rise globally. Retail investors viewed this uprise with high optimism, putting aside previous falls caused by weak property demand and low steel output level. Prices of iron ore climbed above US$109 per Mt in early October, a week after the announcement of the economic stimulus measures by the PBOC. This gain reflected the bullish sentiment towards the demand outlook for iron ore vis-à-vis the stimulus measures generally and home buying relaxation rules and lower mortgage rates specifically. BHP’s iron ore sales realized an average price of US$101.04 per Mt in FY2024, which notably is slightly below iron ore's current price level in the global market. In our opinion, BHP will benefit from the short-term momentum in iron ore price movements relating to the above-mentioned stimulus measures. Indeed, BHP’s share price has reacted to such news positively, climbing steadily from the price of about US$55 per share (in NYSE) in mid/late September to as high as US$62.43 per share in early October, reflecting a bullish sentiment on the prospects of iron ore demands as well as the positive impact on BHP’s earnings from the iron ore segment in the coming year.

Risk Assessment

Cyclical Factors

The company have been facing challenges in the coal and nickel sectors, and with temporary suspension of nickel production and the sharp decline in steelmaking coal production, these cyclical factors have resulted in corresponding revenue drops. The percentage weights of these drops did not materially affect the overall performance for FY2024. However, in our opinion, concerns about the company's long-term prospect in these areas, especially as global trends shift away from fossil fuels, cannot be ignored.

China Factor

It is unclear whether, and if so to what extent, China’s stimulus measures will be implemented, and if so, to what extent. Even if the stimulus measures shall be fully implemented, it remains unclear whether, and for how long, the measures will affect iron ore demands.

Copper Shortage

In our opinion, copper shortage is a double-edged sword. On one hand, supply and demand imbalance will help to drive up equilibrium price in the long run. On the other hand, copper’s perceived demand and the prospect of insufficient copper supply over the long haul may incentivize users to anticipate the risk earlier on and become actively engaged in search for substitution or alternatives.

Valuation Consideration

Note that valuation of a mining company is more an art than a science, as its earnings growth is highly sensitive to economic changes in general and commodities' price movements in particular. Hence, our consideration of "fair value" should not be taken as a target value for predicting share prices, but rather should be interpreted as reference points for discussion purposes.

For simplicity reason, we will consider two sets of growth scenarios, one being a base case (in which BHP is assumed to grow in line with its long-term average growth rate) and another being a bull case (in which BHP is assumed to achieve 2% over and above its long-term average growth rate). Further, our DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) analysis will adopt 2 general assumptions - (i) discount rate of 8.5% and (ii) terminal growth rate of 2% for all relevant periods for both scenarios. Based on these assumptions, and the data reported in the company's FY2024 annual report as well as industry sources, we derive the following parameters:

FCF for base year (as of 30 June 2024) US$11.85 billion
Total outstanding shares (as of 30 June 2024) 5.07 billion
FCF2024 US$2.34 per share
Multiplier X (for Base Case - see below) (1+6%)/(1+8.5%) = 0.9770
Multiplier Y (for Bull Case - see below) (1+8%)/(1+8.5%) = 0.9954
Terminal Value per share (after 30 June 2035) FCF2034 x [(1+2%)/(8.5%-2%)]

Base Case - Assume earnings growth stays at the long-term average growth rate of 6% per annum in the first 10 years, and thereafter grow at a terminal rate of 2% per annum, then (i) DCF for the first 10 years = $20.61; (ii) terminal value for the remainder period = $29.05; and (iii) estimated fair value = $49.66. Details are shown below.

2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030 2031 2032 2033 2034
$2.28 $2.23 $2.18 $2.13 $2.08 $2.03 $1.99 $1.94 $1.89 $1.85
FCF per share from 2025 to 2034 US$20.61
Terminal value per share US$29.05
Fair value per share US$49.66

Bull Case - Assume earnings growth stays at 2% above the long-term average growth rate, i.e. 8% per annum, in the first 10 years, and thereafter grows at a terminal rate of 2% per annum, then (i) DCF for the first 10 years = $22.79; (ii) terminal value for the remainder period = $35.02; and (iii) estimated fair value = $57.81. Details are shown below.

2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030 2031 2032 2033 2034
$2.33 $2.32 $2.31 $2.29 $2.28 $2.27 $2.26 $2.25 $2.24 $2.23
FCF per share from 2025 to 2034 US$22.79
Terminal value per share US$35.02
Fair value per share US$57.81

By way of cross-reference, the fair value per BHP share is estimated at US$54 according to Morningstar Rating, while BHP share is currently trading slightly below US$56. Further, if we also assume that BHP share’s current price has fully reflected current economic climate and commodity supply and demand dynamics, then we opine that any upside from here (the bull case) will be dependent on "positive unknowns", including those two factors outlined above, namely copper demand in the long run and iron ore demand in the short run.

Conclusion

In our opinion, BHP is a well-managed global mining company with a solid track record in financial performance and attractive dividend payout for income investors. Copper and iron ore together contributed over 95% of the company’s latest earnings. In this connection, we opine that BHP’s copper portfolio is a long-term beneficiary of the macro trends in clean energy expansions and AI adoptions, whereas BHP’s iron ore portfolio is a short-term beneficiary of the cyclical factors propelled by China’s economic stimulus package.

Further, in our opinion, the supply and demand imbalance in copper is a risk to the industry but a revenue driver for the company, whereas the range-bound price performance of iron ore in the last few years together with the recent announcement of China’s economic stimulus package provide an upside potential to BHP’s iron ore segment in the immediate coming year. The potash mining segment has not been accounted for in this article. However, in our view, provided that potash production launch remains on course, the company's potash segment, as and when it comes online, will further weigh-in to the upside.

In conclusion, we believe that the company has demonstrated its ability to monetize the secular growth trend in global copper demand and its ongoing strategy to diversify into potash mining as well as to simultaneously expand its copper mining portfolio. We are also mindful about geopolitical tensions that may affect macroeconomic developments, that may negatively impact its various mining segments, namely, iron ore and copper, in particular. Overall, we consider this name as a target for accumulation for dividend income in the short term and price appreciation in the long term.

data.title: South Korea: Disappointing GDP Could Accelerate Bank Of Korea Rate Cut
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

South Korea's economy narrowly avoided a technical recession

The economy expanded by 0.1% quarter-on-quarter seasonally adjusted in the third quarter of the year, recovering from a contraction of -0.2% in the second quarter but missing the market consensus of 0.4% growth.

Details showed that private consumption (0.5% vs -0.2% in the second quarter) and facility investment (6.9% vs -1.2%) rebounded while construction deepened its contraction (-2.8% vs -1.7%).

Government spending continued to rise 0.6%, with increased expenditures on healthcare benefits. The downside surprise mainly came from exports, which contracted by -0.4% (vs 1.2% in the second quarter). IT exports continued to rise but at a slower pace, and vehicle and petrochemical exports declined. Meanwhile, imports grew 1.5% (vs 1.6%), so net exports made a negative contribution (-0.8pp) to overall growth.

Growth is expected to continue, but at a sub-potential level

GDP outlook

The Bank of Korea (BoK) has mentioned that uncertainties have grown over economic growth for 2024 and 2025, which signals that the central bank is likely to revise its GDP forecasts in the November quarterly outlook report. In a separate report, the composite business sentiment index dropped significantly, which gives a cloudy outlook for the coming quarter's growth.

Based on today's result, we lower our annual GDP forecasts for 2024 and 2025 from 2.3% YoY to 2.2% and from 1.6% to 1.5%, respectively. For the current quarter's GDP, we expect a modest growth of 0.4%.

Car exports were down in the previous quarter mainly due to production line maintenance and longer-than-expected labour strikes, thus are likely to rebound while solid IT exports are likely to continue. Imports have grown for the past two quarters, and import growth in the previous quarter tends to boost investment for the next quarter, so facility investment is likely to remain positive.

Business sentiment deteriorated further in October, clouding future growth

BoK watch

The BoK delivered a hawkish cut at its October meeting, but was very cautious about signalling any further rate cuts in the near future. But, with weaker-than-expected GDP, the BoK's concerns about slowing growth should grow. We don't expect the BoK to cut rates in November, but the tone of its communications should become more dovish. As inflation is expected to stay below 2% for a while, the BoK's policy focus should shift to support growth.

As for the timing of the next rate action, we currently see an April cut as the base case, but the probability of a January cut increases if there are clearer signs of stabilisation in the housing market. However, we believe that the BoK has limited room to cut rates compared to the US Federal Reserve, so it is difficult for the market to suggest the possibility of further aggressive rate cuts.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: MSTU: Too Risky With Not Much Track Record
data.description:
The intrinsic qualities of Bitcoin as a secure store of value and a long-term inflation hedge are consistent with general macroeconomic patterns of major central banks around the world implementing looser monetary policies and taking on more debt in order to sustain the economy.

One company which has taken advantage of this route to currency debasement is MicroStrategy (MSTR). 4 years ago, MSTR has shifted its focus from its enterprise software business to accumulating Bitcoin. This has made MSTR a proxy for Bitcoin. MSTR currently holds 1.2% of the total Bitcoin supply.

The two charts below show the percentage of price change of Bitcoin and MSTR when MicroStrategy first started acquiring Bitcoin and MicroStrategy's Bitcoin holdings over time.

However, in the past few months, MSTR stock price has considerably outperformed Bitcoin. Year to date, MSTR has returned 227.2% compared to Bitcoin which has returned only 59.52%.

One possible factor to note that the high short interest has created the possibility of a short squeeze that can propel more returns if the stock keeps trending upwards and shorts are forced to cover.

Therefore, will using a leveraged ETF such as T-Rex 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF (BATS:MSTU) be useful in taking advantage of short term short squeezes or just to amplify returns in MSTR and Bitcoin?

It is important to note that leveraged ETFs are mainly designed for short-term momentum traders and not for long-term holding.

Introducing MSTU

T-Rex 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF is managed by REX Shares and was introduced on 18 September 2024. Therefore, the ETF is still new and has not much price history yet. The issuer is REX shares, which also offers a range of other leveraged single-stock ETFs. The primary investment aim of the ETF is to deliver 200% of the daily performance of MSTR, before fees and expenses.

As of 22 October 2024:

Profile MSTU
Fund Inception 18 September 2024
AUM $677.22 Mil
Medium Bid/Ask Spread 0.37%
Expense Ratio 1.05%

For an ETF that is just over a month old, it has done well to accumulate an AUM of $677.22 million. Its expense ratio of 1.05% is pretty much in line with the average expense ratio of leveraged ETFs, which is 1.04%.

Below is a table of the fund holdings as of 22 October 2024:

The chart below compares the percentage price changes of MSTU and MSTR. With only a month's data available, it is too early to make concrete conclusions, as the volatility and compounding effects (as explained in the later section) have yet to make a noticeable effect yet.

How MSTU Works

Since there is not much price history to compare how MSTU moves in correlation to MSTR, it will be important to know how MSTU works before considering to add it to your portfolio.

A leveraged ETF is not suitable for all investors, so understanding how it works, and the potential risks are important.

As mentioned earlier, the T-Rex 2X Long MSTR Daily Target ETF seeks to achieve 200% of the daily performance of MSTR, before fees and expenses. However, it is important to emphasize that the ETF is focused on the daily performance of MSTR and is not intending to deviate to a time period other than a single trading day. Therefore, the return for investors that invest for a time period longer or shorter than a single trading day may not be expected to be 200% of the performance of MSTR.

In the MSTU prospectus, it is stated that the fund's principal strategies will be to “enter into one or more swap agreements with major global financial institutions” whereby the Fund will “invest in swap agreements that provide 200% daily exposure to MSTR equal to at least 80% of its net assets (plus any borrowings for investment purposes).” Additionally, the fund will also hold money market funds and short-term investment-grade debt instruments such as US government securities and repurchase agreements.

At the close of each trading day, the fund advisor will rebalance the portfolio to ensure that the fund's exposure to MSTR is consistent with the ETF's 200% of MSTR investment objective. Therefore, the price movement of MSTR during the day's trading session will impact its portfolio turnover and whether the portfolio needs to be rebalanced. For instance, if the price of MSTR increases during the market trading session, this should result in the rise of the ETF's net assets, thus the ETF's exposure will need to be increased.

Therefore, if one is planning to hold it for a time period longer than a day, the return of MSTU will likely be the result of the compounded return of each trading day over the period of time held. This is likely to be a different result from calculating 200% of MSTR's return for the same time period.

Possible Risks with MSTU Before Investing

One caveat to note that is highlighted in MSTU's prospectus is that the fund does not generally take defensive positions regardless of the market condition; and if the fund's underlying security moves more than 50% on a single trading day that is a direction adverse to the fund, the fund investors will lose all their money.

Considering the volatility of MSTR and Bitcoin, this risk cannot be highlighted enough.

There are also a few other potential risks to consider because of the way MSTU is structured. They are rebalancing risk, sector risk, volatility and compounding risk, and counterparty risk. We will touch on them briefly.

Rebalancing risk is evident as the fund needs to be rebalanced every trading day, there is a risk that the ETF's investment exposure to MSTR may not be correlated to the investment objective. This can result in the ETF being exposed to more leverage risk than it is supposed to.

With regard to sector risk, considering that MSTR is a proxy for Bitcoin. Any negative or positive news for the crypto industry can create additional volatility for MSTR and MSTU. There are no clear regulations on crypto yet, and any hints of regulation news can have an effect.

This could lead to volatility and compounding risk. Volatility can exacerbate the effects of compounding. Due to the daily rebalance, there is a potential effect of compounding. This effect can mean that the performance of MSTU can be drastically different from the performance of MSTR during the same period of time. As the holding period increases, the effect of compounding can be more evident. Therefore, since the fund is just about a month old, there is not much price history to judge the compounding effect over a longer period of time.

Lastly, with regard to counterparty risk, this is due to the fund's strategies to initiate swap agreements to gain 200% daily exposure of MSTR. If the counterparty becomes insolvent or affected by collateral needs, it could affect MSTU and the value of its holdings.

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before making any trades in MSTU. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. Therefore, only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them.

MSTU: Is It Worth The Risk?

Trading or investing in a leverage ETF comes with increased risks. Since there is a potential for higher volatility in MSTR and Bitcoin, which in turn can add risk because of how MSTU is structured, it is simply easier to avoid.

If the fund's underlying security moves more than 50% on a single trading day that is a direction adverse to the fund, the fund investors will lose all their money. That is a statement not to be taken lightly.

Considering the risks involved, it is better to just own or trade MSTR rather than MSTU.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Boeing Workers Reject Contract, Strike Continues
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Boeing (BA) factory workers reject contract, continuing six-week strike. (00:24) Tesla (TSLA) rips 12% gain as investors eye the plan for affordable EVs. (01:27) Singtel to sell Dash to Western Union (WU). (02:30)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast.

Boeing (NYSE:BA) factory workers voted late Wednesday to reject the company's contract offer that would have ended a nearly six-week strike.

The vote was 64% in opposition to the deal, which had offered a 35% wage hike over four years.

The International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers said in a statement after the votes were tallied, "After 10 years of sacrifices, we still have ground to make up, and we're hopeful to do so by resuming negotiations promptly."

Without a deal, Boeing's (BA) factories that build the 737, 767 and 777 jets will remain idled.

The vote came on the same day Boeing (BA) reported a $6.2B Q3 loss and warned it would remain cash flow negative in 2025 after burning through more than $10B in the first nine months of 2024.

After closing 1.76% lower on Wednesday, Boeing is down 2.7% in premarket action.

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) rallied late on Wednesday after breaking a streak of missing EPS estimates with its last four quarterly reports and reiterating that it plans to make EVs affordable.

On the earnings conference call, CEO Elon Musk took a victory lap over what he said was a record quarter. He noted that Tesla (TSLA) just produced its 7 millionth vehicle.

Musk said more affordable models will be produced by Tesla (TSLA) in the first half of 2025. He expects vehicle production to increase by 20% to 30% in 2025. Cybercab production is anticipated to be scaled up aggressively in 2026. Regarding autonomy, the CEO said miles-per-intervention metrics continue to improve, and the company thinks FSD will exceed human-level driving safety by Q2 of 2025.

He said the future of Tesla (TSLA) is autonomous when answering a question if a mass-market $25K vehicle would be introduced.

The Tesla Semi was noted to be preferred by the PepsiCo (PEP) drivers taking part in the all-electric semi initiative.

Tesla (TSLA) is up 11% premarket.

Singtel and Western Union (NYSE:WU) announced a conditional agreement for the sale of Dash, Singapore's all-in-one mobile wallet.

This transaction is part of Singtel's strategy to streamline its operations and focus on core business areas, while Western Union (WU) aims to enhance its digital financial services.

Western Union (WU) is up 1.3% premarket.

More articles on Seeking Alpha:

T-Mobile US delivers robust results, forecast on continued subscriber strength; shares rise

Frontier's biggest holder hires adviser to evaluate sale to Verizon - Bloomberg

The U.S. debt: How annual deficits pile up to a $35T-plus total

Catalyst watch:

  • Notable investor events include Beyond's (BYON) Investor Meeting. The meeting will take place after the company entered into a partnership with The Container Store (TCS) aimed at improving customer experience utilizing both the Bed Bath and Beyond Brand and Container Store brands.

Now let’s take a look at the markets as of 6 am. Ahead of the opening bell today, Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory. Crude oil is up 2% at $72/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.5% at $66,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 0.6% and the DAX is up 0.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Seadrill (SDRL) shares surged 12% following reports of ongoing discussions with Transocean (RIG) about a potential merger.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Chicago Fed National Activity Index

  • 8:30 am Initial Jobless Claims

  • 4:30 pm Fed Balance Sheet
